# Target State Architecture

## Document control

| Field          | Value                                                  |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Project name   | Hospital Admissions Analytics Platform Azure Migration |
| Document title | Target State Architecture                              |
| Version        | v0.1                                                   |
| Document owner | M S M Marasinghe                                       |
| Last updated   | 2026-05-28                                             |
| Status         | Approved                                               |

## Business purpose

This document defines the Azure target-state architecture for the Hospital Admissions Analytics Platform. Its purpose is to modernize ingestion, storage, transformation, quality, and serving while preserving the business definitions, historical behavior, and reporting trust established by the legacy warehouse.

The design must support a hospital environment where analytics are trust-sensitive, privacy-sensitive, and budget-sensitive. That means the solution should be practical, defensible, and simpler than a generic enterprise cloud blueprint.

## Design principles

The architecture is guided by a small set of design principles.

First, preserve business meaning before optimizing tooling. The migration must keep the same admissions, patient, doctor, location, and reporting semantics that the legacy platform already supports.

Second, separate layers cleanly. Raw ingestion, standardization, transformation, quality testing, and report serving should each have a clear responsibility so the design stays understandable and maintainable.

Third, favor managed, cost-aware services. The hospital context does not justify unnecessary platform sprawl, overbuilt streaming components, or specialized services that add cost without business value.

Fourth, make validation part of the platform. The migration should not assume data is correct merely because it was loaded successfully; it must be testable and observable.

Fifth, design for future change without overcommitting now. The platform should allow later extension to other domains, but the first version should remain focused on admissions analytics.

## Legacy-to-Azure component mapping

| Legacy component                     | Azure counterpart                                        | Design rationale                                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Patient, doctor, location file feeds | Azure Data Factory ingestion into ADLS Gen2              | File feeds can be landed reliably with batch orchestration and explicit zone handling.                                             |
| Admission SQL Server source          | Azure Data Factory extraction into ADLS Gen2             | The on-prem database fits a scheduled batch extraction pattern and can be lifted into the same landing model.                      |
| SSIS orchestration                   | Azure Data Factory pipeline orchestration                | ADF can coordinate source extraction, dependency order, and downstream loads without reproducing SSIS package complexity verbatim. |
| Staging tables and standardization   | ADLS staged zone and Synapse staging models              | The staged zone provides a clean boundary for standardized, load-ready data before transformation.                                 |
| SQL Server warehouse                 | Azure Synapse Analytics                                  | Synapse becomes the central analytical warehouse while preserving the dimensional business model.                                  |
| Stored procedure transformations     | dbt models and SQL transformations in Synapse            | dbt makes transformation logic version-controlled, testable, and easier to reason about than opaque package logic.                 |
| DimLocation SCD Type 2 logic         | dbt snapshots or incremental SCD handling                | Historical location context must be preserved explicitly and testably.                                                             |
| SSAS semantic layer                  | Synapse views / marts plus Power BI semantic consumption | The target should preserve semantic behavior while reducing reliance on an additional on-prem cube tier.                           |
| Excel pivot usage                    | Excel connected to the new semantic layer                | Business users should retain familiar ad hoc exploration patterns.                                                                 |
| Power BI reports                     | Power BI on Synapse views or marts                       | Report serving should remain business-friendly while pointing to the new trusted warehouse model.                                  |

## High-level target architecture

The target architecture uses Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 as the landing and storage backbone, Azure Data Factory for orchestration, Azure Synapse Analytics as the central warehouse, dbt for transformation logic, and Power BI as the primary serving layer.

Operational sources remain external to the platform. File-based patient, doctor, and location feeds plus admission data from SQL Server are ingested into ADLS, standardized through staged processing, transformed into warehouse models in Synapse, and then served through Power BI and Excel-facing semantic outputs.

This design keeps the platform simple enough for a hospital analytics workload while still supporting traceability from source to report.

## Service flow

The operational flow is deliberately linear: ADF lands source files and SQL extracts into ADLS raw, standardization produces staged data, dbt transforms staged data into Synapse warehouse models, and Power BI consumes the trusted outputs through views or marts.

This sequence mirrors the legacy logic without copying SSIS package complexity directly. It also keeps each layer accountable for one job, which is valuable for debugging, governance, and cost control.

## Data zones and storage design

ADLS Gen2 is organized into three primary zones: raw, staged, and curated.

The raw zone stores source extracts exactly as received. This provides a durable audit trail and makes it easier to reprocess data if transformations change.

The staged zone holds lightly standardized data, such as cleaned files, normalized formats, and source-aligned structures. This zone is useful for validation and for separating source irregularities from business modeling concerns.

The curated zone contains analytics-ready outputs or warehouse-aligned datasets that are safe for reporting and downstream consumption. For this project, curated outputs should align to the admissions model rather than trying to become a generic enterprise data lake product.

## Ingestion and orchestration design

Azure Data Factory is the orchestration backbone. It should schedule and coordinate extraction from the patient, doctor, location, and admission sources and land those extracts into the correct ADLS zones.

For file feeds, ADF should ingest from batch exports on an agreed schedule and move files into the raw zone first. For the SQL Server admissions source, ADF should use a batch extraction pattern that respects operational windows and preserves repeatability.

ADF should also manage dependency order. Source ingest should happen before staging standardization, staging should happen before warehouse transformation, and warehouse loads should happen before semantic serving refreshes.

The design does not require streaming or near-real-time ingestion. The legacy platform is batch-oriented, and the report does not justify adding streaming complexity for this first target-state version.

If file delivery or database extraction timing changes later, the orchestration layer can be adjusted without changing the overall architecture principle. That flexibility is useful for a migration where source cadence is not yet fully documented.

## Warehouse and transformation design

Azure Synapse Analytics is the central analytical warehouse. It should host the dimensional model for `FactAdmission`, `DimPatient`, `DimDoctor`, `DimLocation`, and `DimDate` in a structure that preserves the legacy business grain and reporting meaning.

dbt should implement staging models, dimensional models, and fact models in a version-controlled manner. This makes the transformation logic reviewable and testable, which is especially important because the legacy ETL embeds business logic in SSIS and stored procedures.

SCD Type 2 handling for `DimLocation` should be implemented explicitly in dbt using snapshots or incremental logic that preserves effective dating and current-record behavior. `FactAdmission` should remain one row per hospital admission and should preserve the accumulating transaction metrics used in the legacy design.

Synapse views or marts should provide the business-facing layer that Power BI and Excel can consume without exposing raw warehouse complexity.

## Data quality and observability design

Data quality should be implemented as a first-class part of the target platform. dbt tests should validate not-null rules, uniqueness, relationship integrity, valid date logic, and business-specific checks such as non-negative durations.

Observability should include pipeline execution visibility, load status, failure points, and basic lineage from source to curated output. The goal is not enterprise theater; it is to ensure the hospital can tell whether a refresh succeeded and whether the outputs are trustworthy.

Quality checks should run before reports are refreshed so the semantic layer does not publish questionable data. This is especially important in healthcare analytics, where trust can be damaged by silent data issues even if the platform technically runs.

## Security and privacy assumptions

The platform must handle healthcare data with appropriate privacy and security controls. At a minimum, access should be role-based, evidence should be portfolio-safe, and any sample data used for demonstration should avoid exposing patient-level sensitive information.

The target architecture assumes that the project does not need open public access to raw or curated data. The design should favor controlled access patterns, least-privilege permissions, and separation between working data and presentation assets.

Because this is a healthcare case study, privacy is not a side issue. It is a core design constraint that affects storage, sharing, screenshots, and validation artifacts.

## Cost-aware design decisions

The platform should stay intentionally lean. ADLS Gen2, ADF, Synapse, dbt, and Power BI cover the core needs without adding unnecessary services that increase cost or operational burden.

The design avoids overengineering with streaming, multiple warehouses, or extra orchestration layers because the reported workload is batch-based and business-driven rather than high-frequency operational telemetry.

Managed and consumption-based services should be preferred where they do not weaken the business model. The hospital’s budget sensitivity means the architecture must justify every service by its contribution to reliability, maintainability, or reporting trust.

## Open questions and risks

- Exact on-prem source access patterns, file delivery schedules, and SQL extraction windows are not yet confirmed.
- The detailed target security model, including workspace and data-access roles, still needs to be finalized.
- The report confirms the Azure pattern but does not require a specific Synapse deployment model, so that design decision remains open.
- Whether Power BI should connect directly to Synapse views or to a separate semantic artifact still needs validation.
- The final form of SCD Type 2 implementation in dbt needs to be confirmed against the actual source history and business rule behavior.
- Cost estimates are still directional; the project will need a later sizing step before any final implementation commitment.
- Any future extension to billing, labs, or pharmacy is intentionally out of scope for this first architecture version.

## Evidence references

- Hospital Admissions Analytics Platform legacy architecture and Azure migration proposal.
- Legacy assignment report describing the source systems, warehouse model, SSIS behavior, semantic layer, and Power BI/Excel usage.
- Future evidence should include the target architecture diagram, Azure service mapping notes, dbt model inventory, and a validation checklist once implementation begins.
